title: You Don't Have to Have the Words
content: People ask me all the time what to say to someone in a hospital bed. Most of the time the right answer is: nothing. You pull up a chair, you sit close, and you let them know they are not alone. Presence is the ministry. Words can wait.
excerpt: Sometimes the most powerful thing you can offer is simply staying.

title: Saturday Morning at 7: Why I Still Love Live Radio
content: Every Saturday I am in the WGSP studio on Beale Street by 5:30 AM. The city is still quiet. I make my notes, say a prayer over the mic, and then for two hours I get to speak directly to Memphis. No algorithm, no delay, no filter. Just voice and community. That is a gift I do not take lightly.
excerpt: What live radio on WGSP 1490 AM still means to me after seven years.

title: The Ministry of Ordinary Days
content: Not every day has a dramatic moment. Most chaplaincy is ordinary - walking the halls, checking in, being a steady presence. Psalm 23 does not say the Lord leads us through the valley of constant crisis. There is a table prepared in the presence of our enemies, yes, but also still waters. Both are the work.
excerpt: Chaplaincy is built on ordinary faithfulness, not dramatic moments.

title: Episode Recap: Songs That Carry Us Through Winter
content: This week on Morning Reflections we opened with Lauren Daigle's 'You Say' and closed with Keith and Kristyn Getty's 'In Christ Alone.' In between, several listeners called in with the songs that got them through the hardest winters of their lives. Patsy Cline came up more than once. So did James Taylor. Music is one of God's more generous gifts.
excerpt: Listener favorites from the episode on music, memory, and surviving winter.

title: Grief Is Not a Problem to Solve
content: In the ICU we sometimes talk about the difference between fixing and healing. Fixing is what surgeons do. Healing is larger. It includes the person who does not recover the way anyone hoped. Grief is part of that larger healing. When we rush people through it - or rush ourselves - we miss something essential about what it means to love someone.
excerpt: Why rushing grief is a mistake, and what it means to sit with loss instead.

title: Episode Recap: Finding Rest in a Restless Season
content: This Saturday's show focused on rest - real rest, not just sleep. We talked about Sabbath as an act of trust. If you missed it, the key question I asked listeners: when was the last time you did something that had no purpose except delight? For me it is sitting on the porch swing with Mercy and a cup of coffee before the neighborhood wakes up. That counts.
excerpt: Saturday's episode on Sabbath, rest, and what it means to truly stop.

title: What I Learned from 16 Years on the Oncology Floor
content: I have been a chaplain at Bluff City Medical Center since October 2010. The oncology unit has been part of my rotation the entire time. I will not share patient stories - that door stays closed. But I can tell you what those years have taught me about what matters: people want to be known, not just cared for. There is a difference.
excerpt: Lessons from sixteen years of chaplaincy on the oncology floor at Bluff City Medical Center.

title: New Year Sunrise: A Meditation on New Beginnings
content: Every New Year I deliver the dawn meditation at Grace Community Church. This year I spoke on John 1 - the light that shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it. I love that image. New life arrives quietly, like light easing over the horizon. That is Memphis in the cold of January. That is most of what redemption looks like.
excerpt: The meditation I delivered at Grace Community Church New Year sunrise service.

title: Episode Recap: Mothers, Daughters, and the Long Arc of Forgiveness
content: Our Honoring the Women of Faith show is always one of our biggest. Listeners called in from all over the Mid-South about complicated mother relationships - the ones that needed more than a Hallmark card. We read from Proverbs 31, but we also held space for people whose mothers were absent, difficult, or already gone. Brenda Brown would say the stories we share are the ones that crack us open. I believe that.
excerpt: The Honoring the Women of Faith episode on complicated love, forgiveness, and the long view.

title: Draft: On Compassion Fatigue and Knowing When to Ask for Help
content: I have been sitting with this post for a while. Caregivers are not supposed to admit they are tired. Chaplains are supposed to be reservoirs, not rivers. But a reservoir that is never replenished eventually runs dry. I am still figuring out how to write this one without it sounding like I have it all figured out - because I do not.
excerpt: A draft post on burnout, self-care, and what caregivers need to hear.

title: A Prayer for the City of Memphis
content: We read this prayer on the air last Saturday and the response was overwhelming. I am posting it here so you can keep it. Pray it over your neighborhood, your hospital, your school. Memphis is a city that knows how to survive. Let's ask for more than survival. Let's ask for flourishing.
excerpt: A prayer for Memphis that listeners asked me to post after the broadcast.

title: Episode Recap: When the Diagnosis Changes Everything
content: Last Saturday I spoke with Rev. Angela Dawson, chaplain at Golden Heart Medical Center, about what happens to a person's faith when a diagnosis arrives. We talked about the shock, the bargaining, and - slowly - the reorientation. Angela said something I keep returning to: a diagnosis is not the end of your story. It is a chapter. Sometimes it is even the one where the real story begins.
excerpt: A conversation with Rev. Angela Dawson on faith, diagnosis, and spiritual reorientation.

title: Community Health Fair at Grace Community: Save the Date
content: Deacon Charles Mosley and I are organizing the annual community health fair at Grace Community Church, set for Saturday, October 3, 2026. This year we are bringing in free blood pressure and glucose screenings, a walk-in chaplaincy station, and resource tables for mental health and nutrition services in South Memphis and Midtown. Details and registration link coming soon.
excerpt: Grace Community Church community health fair on October 3, 2026 - details coming soon.

title: Morning Pages: Why I Write Before the Sun Comes Up
content: My alarm goes off at 4:45 AM. Before coffee, before the phone, before anything else, I open my journal. Morning pages - three pages longhand, whatever is in my head. Julia Cameron calls it unclogging the drain. I call it the difference between surviving the day and actually living it. Some of the best thoughts I have ever had on the radio started on those pages.
excerpt: Why I write three pages by hand before 5 AM every day, and what it does for my ministry.

title: Draft: CPE Continuing Education - What I'm Working Through This Year
content: I have 28 of my required 50 CPE hours completed for the year. Still have 22 to go before December 31. I am considering a fall intensive on trauma-informed spiritual care. Still doing research on which program makes the most sense. Notes to self for now.
excerpt: Notes on this year's CPE continuing education plan and fall intensive options.

title: A Chaplain's Guide to Sitting with Uncertainty
content: Rebecca asked me to write a guest piece, and I am honored. What I have learned in my years at Golden Heart is that uncertainty is not the enemy of faith - it is often where faith becomes real. We do not need answers as much as we need accompaniment. That is what chaplains offer. Not resolution. Presence.
excerpt: Guest post from Rev. Angela Dawson on accompanying people through uncertainty.

title: The Importance of Whole-Person Health in the Church
content: As a nursing professor, I see students who understand the clinical side of care but miss the spiritual dimension entirely. Faith communities can be powerful places of healing - or harm. What Grace Community is doing with their health fair is a model: bringing skilled clinicians and pastoral care workers into the same room, treating people as whole persons. That is the vision I was trained to have.
excerpt: Guest post from Sandra Coles on whole-person health and what the church can offer.
